message.content: Adversary-in-the-Middle: DHCP Spoofing involves adversaries redirecting network traffic by spoofing DHCP traffic and acting as a malicious DHCP server. This allows them to collect network communications, including credentials sent over unencrypted protocols, and perform actions like Network Sniffing or Transmitted Data Manipulation. DHCP operates on a client-server model, providing network configuration settings and allocating network addresses. Adversaries can spoof a rogue DHCP server, causing legitimate hosts to receive malicious configurations, such as adversary-owned DNS servers. This enables adversaries to route client traffic through their systems and gather information. Additionally, adversaries may exploit DHCPv6 by responding to INFORMATION-REQUEST messages with malicious configurations. They can also perform DHCP exhaustion attacks by flooding the network with DISCOVER messages to deplete the DHCP allocation pool. Mitigation strategies include using DHCP snooping to filter untrusted DHCP messages, implementing network segmentation to limit the spread of malicious configurations, and employing strong encryption protocols to protect sensitive communications. Regular network monitoring and anomaly detection can also help identify and respond to suspicious DHCP activities.
